title: Microsoft Graph Security - Permission Planning By Surface
applies_to: Choosing least-privileged Microsoft Graph permissions for security workflows
Preflight planning for Graph security integrations
Separating auth scope selection from endpoint implementation

confirmed_facts.fact: The Microsoft Graph permissions reference marks security permissions as admin-consent-required for both application and delegated modes.

The least-privileged permission for GET /security/alerts_v2 is SecurityAlert.Read.All for both delegated work or school accounts and application access; the higher privilege is SecurityAlert.ReadWrite.All.

The least-privileged permission for GET /security/incidents is SecurityIncident.Read.All for both delegated work or school accounts and application access; the higher privilege is SecurityIncident.ReadWrite.All.

The least-privileged permission for GET /security/secureScores is SecurityEvents.Read.All for both delegated work or school accounts and application access; the higher privilege is SecurityEvents.ReadWrite.All.

The least-privileged permission for POST /security/runHuntingQuery is ThreatHunting.Read.All for both delegated work or school accounts and application access, with no higher privileged permission listed.

The least-privileged permission for Microsoft Graph attack simulation read operations is AttackSimulation.Read.All for both delegated work or school accounts and application access, with personal Microsoft accounts not supported.

implementation_notes: Use this entry for permission preflight and route selection, then defer to the target endpoint page before implementation if the workflow needs write operations or less-common entities.
For automation, pair the application permission from this map with the authorization rules in ms-graph-security-authorization and the token mechanics in ms-graph-oauth2-client-credentials.
For delegated workflows, the least-privileged Graph permission is not sufficient on its own for many security routes; also check the Entra role requirement from ms-graph-security-authorization.

Treat permission planning as route-specific. Do not reuse SecurityEvents.Read.All as a generic permission for all Graph security surfaces just because it covers secure score and some legacy security events.
common_failure_patterns: Using SecurityEvents.Read.All for incidents or alerts_v2 when the target route actually needs SecurityIncident.Read.All or SecurityAlert.Read.All.
Requesting delegated permissions and consent successfully but still failing because the workflow was designed for app-only automation.
Assuming a write-capable permission is required for simple reporting when a read-only permission would have been sufficient.
Testing with a personal Microsoft account even though the target Graph security API surface only supports work or school accounts.
caveats: This entry is a planning shortcut for common security surfaces, not a complete copy of the Microsoft Graph permissions reference.
Write operations and niche security resources can require different permissions than the read routes summarized here.
Some older authorization guidance uses broader legacy permission families; prefer endpoint-specific least-privileged permissions for modern surfaces such as alerts_v2 and incidents.
